get_notable_items(candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path, top_n: int = 3) -> List[NotableItem]

Minor: export get_agent_enrichment_for_records() for reuse by significance_score(). No structural changes to the agent bio pipeline.

Unit tests for thematic matching, significance scoring, and pedagogical framing. No DB or API dependencies.

deliverables.description: New module: 8 pre-authored thematic context entries, theme matching engine, significance scoring function, notable items selector. ~400-500 lines.
Integration of thematic context and notable items into handle_query_definition_phase(). ~30 lines added.
Pedagogical framing functions and theme-aware followup suggestions. ~60 lines added.
Optional thematic_context field on ChatResponse. ~2 lines added.
13 unit tests covering theme matching, significance scoring, and notable items.
2 integration tests for DB-dependent scoring and API response integration.

result.goal: Add scholarly narrative depth to 7 queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q11, Q16, Q18) that currently score 9-15/25 on the historian evaluation due to THIN_NARRATIVE and missing pedagogical framing. Introduce a thematic context layer with pre-authored, cited historical paragraphs; a significance scoring function for highlighting notable items; and pedagogical framing for teaching-oriented queries. All thematic content is deterministic, human-authored prose with citations -- never LLM-generated fabrication.

implementationSteps.description: Create Pydantic models: ThematicBlock (theme_id, title, context_paragraph, citations, teaching_note, matched_by), SignificanceResult (record_id, score, factors), NotableItem (record_id, score, reason, highlight_text). ThematicBlock.context_paragraph is the pre-authored prose. ThematicBlock.citations is a list of scholarly references (author, title, year). ThematicBlock.matched_by records which filter dimensions triggered the match.
implementationSteps.title: Define data models for thematic context

implementationSteps.description: Write 8 pre-authored thematic context entries as a Python dict (THEMATIC_REGISTRY). Each entry is a ThematicEntry dataclass with: theme_id (str), match_rules (list of MatchRule with field/value/date_range conditions), context_paragraph (str, 3-5 sentences of scholarly prose), citations (list of Reference), teaching_note (Optional[str]). The 8 themes are: (1) venetian_hebrew_printing -- Bomberg, Bragadin, Council of Trent, censorship; (2) amsterdam_dutch_jerusalem -- Menasseh ben Israel, Sephardic refugees, freedom of press; (3) christian_hebraism -- Buxtorf, Reuchlin, Pfefferkorn controversy; (4) haskalah -- Mendelssohn, Berlin Haskalah, secularization; (5) incunabula_spread -- Gutenberg impact, first Hebrew printed books, Soncino; (6) talmud_printing -- Bomberg 1520-23, 1553 burning, Slavuta/Vilna; (7) napoleonic_emancipation -- Sanhedrin, ghetto dissolution, secular publishing; (8) ottoman_hebrew_printing -- Constantinople, Salonika, Sephardic diaspora. Each paragraph must clearly cite reference works (e.g., Heller 2004, Offenberg 1990, Fuks 1987) and be labeled as historical context.
implementationSteps.designNotes: These paragraphs are the scholarly heart of E4. They must be authored by hand with domain expertise, not generated by LLM. Each paragraph should be factually verifiable against the cited references. The match_rules use the same FilterField enum from scripts/schemas/query_plan.py to ensure type safety.
implementationSteps.title: Author thematic context entries

implementationSteps.description: Function get_thematic_context(filters: List[Filter], candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path) -> Optional[ThematicBlock]. Algorithm: (1) Extract filter dimensions from the query plan filters (place values, date ranges, subject keywords, publisher values, language). (2) For each ThematicEntry in THEMATIC_REGISTRY, evaluate match_rules against extracted dimensions. A match_rule specifies required field matches (e.g., place_norm IN ['venice'], date_range OVERLAPS 1500-1600, subject CONTAINS 'talmud'). (3) Score each theme by number of matching dimensions. (4) Return the highest-scoring theme if score >= threshold (at least 1 strong match). (5) If multiple themes tie, prefer the one with more matching dimensions. (6) Return None if no theme matches (fail silent -- never fabricate). The matching must be case-insensitive and handle normalized values.
implementationSteps.designNotes: Match rules must be generous enough to catch queries like 'Hebrew books in Venice 16th century' but not so broad that every query triggers a thematic block. The place match should use the same normalized place values as the imprints table (e.g., 'venice' not 'Venice'). Date range overlap uses the same logic as the query executor: theme_date_start <= query_date_end AND theme_date_end >= query_date_start.
implementationSteps.title: Implement theme matching logic

implementationSteps.description: Function significance_score(candidate: Candidate, db_path: Path) -> SignificanceResult. Multi-factor scoring: (A) Date rarity -- pre-1500: +5, 1500-1550: +3, 1550-1600: +2, 1600-1700: +1; uses candidate.date_start. (B) Enrichment richness -- query authority_enrichment for this record's agents; count of fields populated (birth_year, occupations, teachers, students, notable_works, wikipedia_url, image_url); each populated field +0.5, max +3.5. (C) Place rarity -- query imprints table for place_norm frequency across entire DB; places with <10 records: +2, <50: +1. (D) First edition indicators -- check MARC 250 (edition statement) in notes table; absence of edition statement on pre-1700 book: +1 (likely first edition). (E) Subject richness -- count of subject headings; >5: +1. Total score is sum of factors, each factor recorded in SignificanceResult.factors list. This function is SHARED with E5 curation module -- design for reuse. The db_path parameter allows DB lookups for enrichment and frequency data.
implementationSteps.designNotes: significance_score() must be a pure function that takes a Candidate and returns a score. It should NOT modify the candidate. For performance, the function should batch DB lookups when called via get_notable_items(). The factors list provides transparency: each factor is a (name, value, reason) tuple so the response can explain WHY an item is notable. Pre-1500 date weighting reflects the extreme rarity of incunabula in any collection.
implementationSteps.title: Implement significance scoring

implementationSteps.description: Function get_notable_items(candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path, top_n: int = 3) -> List[NotableItem]. Calls significance_score() for each candidate (with batched DB lookups for efficiency). Sorts by descending score. Returns top_n items as NotableItem(record_id, title, score, highlight_reasons: List[str]). The highlight_reasons are human-readable strings like 'Printed before 1500 (incunabulum)', 'Author has rich biographical data (Wikidata)', 'Rare printing location (only 5 records from this city)'. Respects the _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold from narrative_agent.py -- returns empty list for sets >100 to avoid expensive bulk scoring.
implementationSteps.title: Implement get_notable_items()

implementationSteps.description: New function format_teaching_note(thematic_block: ThematicBlock) -> str. Formats the teaching_note field from ThematicBlock with a clear label: '**Teaching note**: {note}'. Also formats citations as a 'Further reading' section: '**Further reading**: {author} ({year}), *{title}*'. Modify generate_followups() to add thematic deep-dive suggestions when a thematic block was matched. For example, if the venetian_hebrew_printing theme matched, suggest 'Compare Venetian printing with Amsterdam' and 'Show me the impact of the Council of Trent on Hebrew publishing'. Add 2-3 theme-specific follow-up templates per thematic entry.
implementationSteps.title: Add pedagogical framing to formatter

implementationSteps.description:
In handle_query_definition_phase(), after the existing agent narrative generation block (lines 588-601), add a new block: (1) Import get_thematic_context and get_notable_items from scripts.chat.thematic_context. (2) Call thematic_block = get_thematic_context(query_plan.filters, candidate_set.candidates, bib_db). (3) If thematic_block is not None, format and append to response_message: '

---
**Historical Context**: {thematic_block.context_paragraph}' followed by format_teaching_note(thematic_block) if teaching_note exists. (4) Call notable_items = get_notable_items(candidate_set.candidates, bib_db) if result_count > 0 and result_count <= 100. (5) If notable_items exist, append '

**Notable items in this set**:' followed by formatted list. (6) Add thematic_block and notable_items to response_metadata dict. (7) If thematic_block has theme-specific followups, merge them with existing suggested_followups (theme-specific first, then generic). All of this is wrapped in try/except like the existing agent narrative block (non-blocking, never fails the response).
implementationSteps.designNotes: The thematic context block is clearly separated from the agent narrative with a horizontal rule and bold 'Historical Context' label. This makes it visually distinct and prevents users from confusing authored context with catalog data. The non-blocking pattern matches the existing agent_narrative try/except pattern at lines 588-601.
implementationSteps.title: Integrate into handle_query_definition_phase()

implementationSteps.description: Add optional thematic_context field to ChatResponse model (after line 201): thematic_context: Optional[str] = None. This allows the frontend to render the thematic block in a dedicated UI section rather than just appending to the message string. Also useful for the metadata dict to carry structured thematic data. No breaking change -- field is optional with None default.
implementationSteps.title: Add thematic_context field to ChatResponse

implementationSteps.description: Test suite covering: (1) Theme matching: verify venetian_hebrew_printing matches filters=[place=venice, year=1500-1600, language=heb]. (2) Theme non-matching: verify no theme matches filters=[place=london, year=1900-1950]. (3) Significance scoring: verify pre-1500 candidate scores higher than 1700 candidate. (4) Notable items: verify top-3 selection from a set of 10 candidates with varying scores. (5) Pedagogical framing: verify format_teaching_note() output contains 'Teaching note' label. (6) Citation formatting: verify Further reading section format. (7) Edge cases: empty candidates, all candidates identical scores, candidates with missing date_start. (8) Threshold respect: verify get_notable_items returns empty for >100 candidates. All tests use mock Candidate objects (no DB dependency for unit tests). Separate integration test file for DB-dependent significance scoring.
implementationSteps.title: Write comprehensive tests

qualityGates.check: Automated test: every ThematicEntry in THEMATIC_REGISTRY has block.citations with len >= 1 and each Reference has non-empty author, title, year
qualityGates.gate: G1: All 8 thematic entries have citations

qualityGates.check: Run get_thematic_context() 100 times with identical filters; verify identical output every time. No randomness, no LLM calls.
Test 10 unrelated filter sets (e.g., place=london year=1950, subject=mathematics) and verify all return None
qualityGates.gate: G3: No false matches on unrelated queries

qualityGates.check: Same candidate always gets same score. No external API calls. Pure function.
Grep the response output for '**Historical Context**' header. Verify thematic paragraph never appears without the label.
Benchmark: theme matching < 1ms, significance scoring for 100 candidates < 20ms, total E4 overhead < 30ms. Measured via pytest-benchmark or manual timing.

Re-run the 7 target queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q11, Q16, Q18) through the API. Verify each response now includes a thematic context paragraph. Manual review by domain expert that narrative quality score improves from current (2-3) to target (4-5).
Full pytest suite passes. The thematic layer is additive -- no existing response format changes. ChatResponse.thematic_context is Optional with None default.
qualityGates.gate: G8: No regression on existing tests

Q1_Bragadin_Venice.whatE4Adds: Thematic context paragraph on Venetian Hebrew printing and censorship. Significance scoring highlights earliest/rarest items in the 16 results. Teaching note contextualizes Bragadin within the rivalry of Venetian presses.
Q1_Bragadin_Venice.whatIsMissing: No context on the Bragadin family's role in Hebrew censorship, the Council of Trent's 1553 Talmud burning decree, or comparison with rival Venetian printers (Bomberg, di Gara, Giustiniani). The narrative agent produces Wikidata bios only.

Q11_Napoleon_era.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Napoleonic emancipation and its publishing impact. Significance scoring highlights transitional works (first secular Hebrew publications). Pedagogical framing on before/after emancipation.
Q11_Napoleon_era.whatIsMissing: 40 results returned with accurate date filtering but no contextual analysis of the Napoleonic era's impact on Jewish publishing -- emancipation debates, dissolution of ghettos, the Sanhedrin of 1807, shift from rabbinic to secular publishing.

Q16_Biblical_commentaries.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on the biblical commentary tradition and the Mikraot Gedolot. Significance scoring ranks by commentator importance and edition rarity. Teaching note for constructing a course on Jewish exegetical tradition.
Q16_Biblical_commentaries.whatIsMissing: Found 11 core biblical commentary texts but limited pedagogical framing -- no explanation of the Mikraot Gedolot tradition, the role of Rashi/Ibn Ezra/Ramban, or the significance of printed commentaries replacing manuscript traditions.

Q18_Talmud_editions.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Talmud printing history from Bomberg to Vilna. Significance scoring highlights landmark editions. Teaching note on using print editions to teach Talmud transmission history.
Q18_Talmud_editions.whatIsMissing: 42 Talmud-related records found with good coverage but no print history significance -- no mention of the Bomberg Talmud (1520-23, first complete printed edition), the 1553 papal burning, the Vilna Shas, or the significance of each major edition.

Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Amsterdam as 'Dutch Jerusalem' with Sephardic community context. Significance scoring surfaces Menasseh's earliest works. Pedagogical note on comparing Amsterdam's freedoms with Venetian censorship.
Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatIsMissing: Found Menasseh ben Israel with a Wikidata bio, but no 'Dutch Jerusalem' framing -- Amsterdam's role as refuge for Sephardic exiles after 1492, the freedom of Hebrew publishing vs Venice's censorship regime.

Q4_Incunabula.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on incunabula and the spread of printing to Jewish communities (Reggio di Calabria 1475, Soncino, Guadalajara). Significance scoring ranks by date (earliest = highest). Teaching note frames the transition from manuscript to print.
Q4_Incunabula.whatIsMissing: Good pre-1500 results (13 records) and narrative identified early printers via bios, but no framing of why incunabula are historically significant -- the spread of printing from Gutenberg, the revolutionary impact on Jewish learning, the rarity and survival rates.

Q5_Constantinople.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Ottoman Hebrew printing with Sephardic diaspora framing. Teaching note compares Ottoman centers (Constantinople, Salonika) with Italian centers (Venice, Rome).
Q5_Constantinople.whatIsMissing: Found 11 Ottoman Hebrew printing records but no comparative context with Venice or Salonika. No mention of why Constantinople became a Hebrew printing center (Sephardic refugees after 1492, Ottoman tolerance).

newDbQueries.sql: SELECT place_norm, COUNT(*) as freq FROM imprints WHERE place_norm IS NOT NULL GROUP BY place_norm
newDbQueries.when: Called once per get_notable_items() invocation, cached in-memory for the batch

newDbQueries.performance: Uses existing index on agents.authority_uri, ~5ms for 100 records
newDbQueries.purpose: Significance scoring: enrichment richness for record agents
newDbQueries.sql: SELECT ae.* FROM agents a JOIN authority_enrichment ae ON a.authority_uri = ae.authority_uri WHERE a.record_id IN (SELECT id FROM records WHERE mms_id IN (?,?,...))
newDbQueries.when: Called once per get_notable_items() invocation with batched record IDs

retrievalChanges.queryPipeline: UNCHANGED -- compile_query(), execute_plan(), build_where_clause() are not modified
retrievalChanges.summary: E4 does NOT change the query compilation or execution pipeline. All changes are post-retrieval: after CandidateSet is produced, thematic context and significance scoring are applied as enrichment layers. The query_plan.filters are read (not modified) to determine which thematic context to attach.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Thematic paragraphs are keyed to universal historical facts (Council of Trent happened in 1545-1563, Bomberg printed the first Talmud in 1520-23). They describe the historical context, not the collection contents. The match_rules reference normalized field values that are stable. Annual review recommended but not urgent.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic paragraphs become stale if collection changes significantly

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: All thematic blocks are clearly labeled with '**Historical Context**:' header and separated by horizontal rule. Citations to reference works reinforce that this is scholarly commentary, not catalog metadata. The teaching note uses explicit 'Teaching note:' prefix.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Users confuse thematic context with catalog data

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Return only the highest-scoring theme (most matching dimensions). If tied, prefer the more specific theme (more match_rules). In the future, could return multiple blocks, but for E4 scope, single-theme is cleaner and avoids information overload.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Multiple themes match the same query (e.g., 'Talmud printed in Venice 16th century' matches both venetian_hebrew_printing and talmud_printing)

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Respect _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold. For sets >100, skip significance scoring entirely (return empty notable items list). The batched DB queries ensure at most 2 additional queries per response. Benchmarked: ~8ms for 100 records on SQLite.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Significance scoring is expensive for large result sets

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Theme matching is pure Python dict lookup against the filters -- no DB or API calls. ~0.1ms per match evaluation. Significance scoring involves 2 DB queries but only for result sets <=100 and only if a theme matched. Total additional latency: <15ms in worst case.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic context adds latency to every query response

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: This is by design. The 8 themes are chosen to address the specific 7 queries flagged in the historian evaluation. The system gracefully returns None when no theme matches (existing behavior preserved). New themes can be added incrementally as simple dict entries -- no code changes needed.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Only 8 themes authored initially -- many queries will not match

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Teaching notes only appear when the thematic block includes a teaching_note (not all themes need one). The note is factual and suggestive ('This set illustrates...'), not prescriptive. Future: could be gated by a user preference flag.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Pedagogical framing may feel inappropriate for non-academic users

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: significance_score() is designed as a standalone pure function with a clean interface (Candidate + db_path -> SignificanceResult). No internal state, no side effects. E5 can import and extend it. The SignificanceFactor model provides extensibility -- E5 can add new factor types without modifying E4 code.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: E5 (curation) depends on significance_score() from this module

fields.matched_by: List[str] -- which filter dimensions triggered the match (e.g., ['place:venice', 'year:1500-1600'])
fields.title: str -- display title (e.g., 'Venetian Hebrew Printing and Censorship')

fields.author: str -- e.g., 'Heller, Marvin J.'
fields.title: str -- e.g., 'The Sixteenth Century Hebrew Book'

fields.date_end: Optional[int] -- maximum year for date range overlap
fields.date_start: Optional[int] -- minimum year for date range overlap
fields.require_all: bool -- if True, all values must appear in filters (AND); if False, any value suffices (OR). Default: False

fields.block: ThematicBlock -- the content to return when matched
fields.match_rules: List[MatchRule] -- conditions that must be met to trigger this theme
fields.min_match_score: int -- minimum number of match_rules that must pass. Default: 1

schemaChanges.change: Add field: thematic_context: Optional[str] = None after line 201. Non-breaking -- optional field with None default.

affectedComponents.purpose: Core new module. Contains pre-authored thematic context paragraphs keyed by (subject, place, date_range) tuples. Pure function: get_thematic_context(filters, result_set) -> Optional[str]. Also contains significance_score() for reuse by E5 curation.

affectedComponents.changeLocation: Lines 588-634 (after agent narrative generation, before response construction)
affectedComponents.purpose: Integrate thematic context into handle_query_definition_phase(). Call get_thematic_context() after query execution (line ~588-604), append thematic block after agent_narrative. Add notable items to response metadata.

affectedComponents.changeLocation: New function format_teaching_note(); modify generate_followups() around line 82-148
affectedComponents.purpose: Add pedagogical framing wrapper. New function format_teaching_note() called from format_for_chat() when thematic context includes a teaching note. Modify generate_followups() to suggest thematic deep-dives.
Minor: export get_agent_enrichment_for_records() for reuse by significance_score(). No structural changes to the agent bio pipeline.

affectedComponents.changeLocation: New models after line 202; optional field on ChatResponse
affectedComponents.purpose: Add ThematicBlock and NotableItem to ChatResponse.metadata schema. Add optional thematic_context field to ChatResponse.
Unit tests for thematic matching, significance scoring, and pedagogical framing. No DB or API dependencies.
Unit tests for pedagogical framing in formatter output.

deliverables.description: New module: 8 pre-authored thematic context entries, theme matching engine, significance scoring function, notable items selector. ~400-500 lines.
Integration of thematic context and notable items into handle_query_definition_phase(). ~30 lines added.
Pedagogical framing functions and theme-aware followup suggestions. ~60 lines added.
Optional thematic_context field on ChatResponse. ~2 lines added.
13 unit tests covering theme matching, significance scoring, and notable items.
2 integration tests for DB-dependent scoring and API response integration.

value.goal: Add scholarly narrative depth to 7 queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q11, Q16, Q18) that currently score 9-15/25 on the historian evaluation due to THIN_NARRATIVE and missing pedagogical framing. Introduce a thematic context layer with pre-authored, cited historical paragraphs; a significance scoring function for highlighting notable items; and pedagogical framing for teaching-oriented queries. All thematic content is deterministic, human-authored prose with citations -- never LLM-generated fabrication.

implementationSteps.description: Create Pydantic models: ThematicBlock (theme_id, title, context_paragraph, citations, teaching_note, matched_by), SignificanceResult (record_id, score, factors), NotableItem (record_id, score, reason, highlight_text). ThematicBlock.context_paragraph is the pre-authored prose. ThematicBlock.citations is a list of scholarly references (author, title, year). ThematicBlock.matched_by records which filter dimensions triggered the match.
implementationSteps.title: Define data models for thematic context

implementationSteps.description: Write 8 pre-authored thematic context entries as a Python dict (THEMATIC_REGISTRY). Each entry is a ThematicEntry dataclass with: theme_id (str), match_rules (list of MatchRule with field/value/date_range conditions), context_paragraph (str, 3-5 sentences of scholarly prose), citations (list of Reference), teaching_note (Optional[str]). The 8 themes are: (1) venetian_hebrew_printing -- Bomberg, Bragadin, Council of Trent, censorship; (2) amsterdam_dutch_jerusalem -- Menasseh ben Israel, Sephardic refugees, freedom of press; (3) christian_hebraism -- Buxtorf, Reuchlin, Pfefferkorn controversy; (4) haskalah -- Mendelssohn, Berlin Haskalah, secularization; (5) incunabula_spread -- Gutenberg impact, first Hebrew printed books, Soncino; (6) talmud_printing -- Bomberg 1520-23, 1553 burning, Slavuta/Vilna; (7) napoleonic_emancipation -- Sanhedrin, ghetto dissolution, secular publishing; (8) ottoman_hebrew_printing -- Constantinople, Salonika, Sephardic diaspora. Each paragraph must clearly cite reference works (e.g., Heller 2004, Offenberg 1990, Fuks 1987) and be labeled as historical context.
implementationSteps.designNotes: These paragraphs are the scholarly heart of E4. They must be authored by hand with domain expertise, not generated by LLM. Each paragraph should be factually verifiable against the cited references. The match_rules use the same FilterField enum from scripts/schemas/query_plan.py to ensure type safety.
implementationSteps.title: Author thematic context entries

implementationSteps.description: Function get_thematic_context(filters: List[Filter], candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path) -> Optional[ThematicBlock]. Algorithm: (1) Extract filter dimensions from the query plan filters (place values, date ranges, subject keywords, publisher values, language). (2) For each ThematicEntry in THEMATIC_REGISTRY, evaluate match_rules against extracted dimensions. A match_rule specifies required field matches (e.g., place_norm IN ['venice'], date_range OVERLAPS 1500-1600, subject CONTAINS 'talmud'). (3) Score each theme by number of matching dimensions. (4) Return the highest-scoring theme if score >= threshold (at least 1 strong match). (5) If multiple themes tie, prefer the one with more matching dimensions. (6) Return None if no theme matches (fail silent -- never fabricate). The matching must be case-insensitive and handle normalized values.
implementationSteps.designNotes: Match rules must be generous enough to catch queries like 'Hebrew books in Venice 16th century' but not so broad that every query triggers a thematic block. The place match should use the same normalized place values as the imprints table (e.g., 'venice' not 'Venice'). Date range overlap uses the same logic as the query executor: theme_date_start <= query_date_end AND theme_date_end >= query_date_start.
implementationSteps.title: Implement theme matching logic

implementationSteps.description: Function significance_score(candidate: Candidate, db_path: Path) -> SignificanceResult. Multi-factor scoring: (A) Date rarity -- pre-1500: +5, 1500-1550: +3, 1550-1600: +2, 1600-1700: +1; uses candidate.date_start. (B) Enrichment richness -- query authority_enrichment for this record's agents; count of fields populated (birth_year, occupations, teachers, students, notable_works, wikipedia_url, image_url); each populated field +0.5, max +3.5. (C) Place rarity -- query imprints table for place_norm frequency across entire DB; places with <10 records: +2, <50: +1. (D) First edition indicators -- check MARC 250 (edition statement) in notes table; absence of edition statement on pre-1700 book: +1 (likely first edition). (E) Subject richness -- count of subject headings; >5: +1. Total score is sum of factors, each factor recorded in SignificanceResult.factors list. This function is SHARED with E5 curation module -- design for reuse. The db_path parameter allows DB lookups for enrichment and frequency data.
implementationSteps.designNotes: significance_score() must be a pure function that takes a Candidate and returns a score. It should NOT modify the candidate. For performance, the function should batch DB lookups when called via get_notable_items(). The factors list provides transparency: each factor is a (name, value, reason) tuple so the response can explain WHY an item is notable. Pre-1500 date weighting reflects the extreme rarity of incunabula in any collection.
implementationSteps.title: Implement significance scoring

implementationSteps.description: Function get_notable_items(candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path, top_n: int = 3) -> List[NotableItem]. Calls significance_score() for each candidate (with batched DB lookups for efficiency). Sorts by descending score. Returns top_n items as NotableItem(record_id, title, score, highlight_reasons: List[str]). The highlight_reasons are human-readable strings like 'Printed before 1500 (incunabulum)', 'Author has rich biographical data (Wikidata)', 'Rare printing location (only 5 records from this city)'. Respects the _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold from narrative_agent.py -- returns empty list for sets >100 to avoid expensive bulk scoring.
implementationSteps.title: Implement get_notable_items()

implementationSteps.description: New function format_teaching_note(thematic_block: ThematicBlock) -> str. Formats the teaching_note field from ThematicBlock with a clear label: '**Teaching note**: {note}'. Also formats citations as a 'Further reading' section: '**Further reading**: {author} ({year}), *{title}*'. Modify generate_followups() to add thematic deep-dive suggestions when a thematic block was matched. For example, if the venetian_hebrew_printing theme matched, suggest 'Compare Venetian printing with Amsterdam' and 'Show me the impact of the Council of Trent on Hebrew publishing'. Add 2-3 theme-specific follow-up templates per thematic entry.
implementationSteps.title: Add pedagogical framing to formatter

implementationSteps.description:
In handle_query_definition_phase(), after the existing agent narrative generation block (lines 588-601), add a new block: (1) Import get_thematic_context and get_notable_items from scripts.chat.thematic_context. (2) Call thematic_block = get_thematic_context(query_plan.filters, candidate_set.candidates, bib_db). (3) If thematic_block is not None, format and append to response_message: '

---
**Historical Context**: {thematic_block.context_paragraph}' followed by format_teaching_note(thematic_block) if teaching_note exists. (4) Call notable_items = get_notable_items(candidate_set.candidates, bib_db) if result_count > 0 and result_count <= 100. (5) If notable_items exist, append '

**Notable items in this set**:' followed by formatted list. (6) Add thematic_block and notable_items to response_metadata dict. (7) If thematic_block has theme-specific followups, merge them with existing suggested_followups (theme-specific first, then generic). All of this is wrapped in try/except like the existing agent narrative block (non-blocking, never fails the response).
implementationSteps.designNotes: The thematic context block is clearly separated from the agent narrative with a horizontal rule and bold 'Historical Context' label. This makes it visually distinct and prevents users from confusing authored context with catalog data. The non-blocking pattern matches the existing agent_narrative try/except pattern at lines 588-601.
implementationSteps.title: Integrate into handle_query_definition_phase()

implementationSteps.description: Add optional thematic_context field to ChatResponse model (after line 201): thematic_context: Optional[str] = None. This allows the frontend to render the thematic block in a dedicated UI section rather than just appending to the message string. Also useful for the metadata dict to carry structured thematic data. No breaking change -- field is optional with None default.
implementationSteps.title: Add thematic_context field to ChatResponse

implementationSteps.description: Test suite covering: (1) Theme matching: verify venetian_hebrew_printing matches filters=[place=venice, year=1500-1600, language=heb]. (2) Theme non-matching: verify no theme matches filters=[place=london, year=1900-1950]. (3) Significance scoring: verify pre-1500 candidate scores higher than 1700 candidate. (4) Notable items: verify top-3 selection from a set of 10 candidates with varying scores. (5) Pedagogical framing: verify format_teaching_note() output contains 'Teaching note' label. (6) Citation formatting: verify Further reading section format. (7) Edge cases: empty candidates, all candidates identical scores, candidates with missing date_start. (8) Threshold respect: verify get_notable_items returns empty for >100 candidates. All tests use mock Candidate objects (no DB dependency for unit tests). Separate integration test file for DB-dependent significance scoring.
implementationSteps.title: Write comprehensive tests

qualityGates.check: Automated test: every ThematicEntry in THEMATIC_REGISTRY has block.citations with len >= 1 and each Reference has non-empty author, title, year
qualityGates.gate: G1: All 8 thematic entries have citations

qualityGates.check: Run get_thematic_context() 100 times with identical filters; verify identical output every time. No randomness, no LLM calls.
Test 10 unrelated filter sets (e.g., place=london year=1950, subject=mathematics) and verify all return None
qualityGates.gate: G3: No false matches on unrelated queries

qualityGates.check: Same candidate always gets same score. No external API calls. Pure function.
Grep the response output for '**Historical Context**' header. Verify thematic paragraph never appears without the label.
Benchmark: theme matching < 1ms, significance scoring for 100 candidates < 20ms, total E4 overhead < 30ms. Measured via pytest-benchmark or manual timing.

Re-run the 7 target queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q11, Q16, Q18) through the API. Verify each response now includes a thematic context paragraph. Manual review by domain expert that narrative quality score improves from current (2-3) to target (4-5).
Full pytest suite passes. The thematic layer is additive -- no existing response format changes. ChatResponse.thematic_context is Optional with None default.
qualityGates.gate: G8: No regression on existing tests

Q1_Bragadin_Venice.whatE4Adds: Thematic context paragraph on Venetian Hebrew printing and censorship. Significance scoring highlights earliest/rarest items in the 16 results. Teaching note contextualizes Bragadin within the rivalry of Venetian presses.
Q1_Bragadin_Venice.whatIsMissing: No context on the Bragadin family's role in Hebrew censorship, the Council of Trent's 1553 Talmud burning decree, or comparison with rival Venetian printers (Bomberg, di Gara, Giustiniani). The narrative agent produces Wikidata bios only.

Q11_Napoleon_era.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Napoleonic emancipation and its publishing impact. Significance scoring highlights transitional works (first secular Hebrew publications). Pedagogical framing on before/after emancipation.
Q11_Napoleon_era.whatIsMissing: 40 results returned with accurate date filtering but no contextual analysis of the Napoleonic era's impact on Jewish publishing -- emancipation debates, dissolution of ghettos, the Sanhedrin of 1807, shift from rabbinic to secular publishing.

Q16_Biblical_commentaries.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on the biblical commentary tradition and the Mikraot Gedolot. Significance scoring ranks by commentator importance and edition rarity. Teaching note for constructing a course on Jewish exegetical tradition.
Q16_Biblical_commentaries.whatIsMissing: Found 11 core biblical commentary texts but limited pedagogical framing -- no explanation of the Mikraot Gedolot tradition, the role of Rashi/Ibn Ezra/Ramban, or the significance of printed commentaries replacing manuscript traditions.

Q18_Talmud_editions.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Talmud printing history from Bomberg to Vilna. Significance scoring highlights landmark editions. Teaching note on using print editions to teach Talmud transmission history.
Q18_Talmud_editions.whatIsMissing: 42 Talmud-related records found with good coverage but no print history significance -- no mention of the Bomberg Talmud (1520-23, first complete printed edition), the 1553 papal burning, the Vilna Shas, or the significance of each major edition.

Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Amsterdam as 'Dutch Jerusalem' with Sephardic community context. Significance scoring surfaces Menasseh's earliest works. Pedagogical note on comparing Amsterdam's freedoms with Venetian censorship.
Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatIsMissing: Found Menasseh ben Israel with a Wikidata bio, but no 'Dutch Jerusalem' framing -- Amsterdam's role as refuge for Sephardic exiles after 1492, the freedom of Hebrew publishing vs Venice's censorship regime.

Q4_Incunabula.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on incunabula and the spread of printing to Jewish communities (Reggio di Calabria 1475, Soncino, Guadalajara). Significance scoring ranks by date (earliest = highest). Teaching note frames the transition from manuscript to print.
Q4_Incunabula.whatIsMissing: Good pre-1500 results (13 records) and narrative identified early printers via bios, but no framing of why incunabula are historically significant -- the spread of printing from Gutenberg, the revolutionary impact on Jewish learning, the rarity and survival rates.

Q5_Constantinople.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Ottoman Hebrew printing with Sephardic diaspora framing. Teaching note compares Ottoman centers (Constantinople, Salonika) with Italian centers (Venice, Rome).
Q5_Constantinople.whatIsMissing: Found 11 Ottoman Hebrew printing records but no comparative context with Venice or Salonika. No mention of why Constantinople became a Hebrew printing center (Sephardic refugees after 1492, Ottoman tolerance).

newDbQueries.sql: SELECT place_norm, COUNT(*) as freq FROM imprints WHERE place_norm IS NOT NULL GROUP BY place_norm
newDbQueries.when: Called once per get_notable_items() invocation, cached in-memory for the batch

newDbQueries.performance: Uses existing index on agents.authority_uri, ~5ms for 100 records
newDbQueries.purpose: Significance scoring: enrichment richness for record agents
newDbQueries.sql: SELECT ae.* FROM agents a JOIN authority_enrichment ae ON a.authority_uri = ae.authority_uri WHERE a.record_id IN (SELECT id FROM records WHERE mms_id IN (?,?,...))
newDbQueries.when: Called once per get_notable_items() invocation with batched record IDs

retrievalChanges.queryPipeline: UNCHANGED -- compile_query(), execute_plan(), build_where_clause() are not modified
retrievalChanges.summary: E4 does NOT change the query compilation or execution pipeline. All changes are post-retrieval: after CandidateSet is produced, thematic context and significance scoring are applied as enrichment layers. The query_plan.filters are read (not modified) to determine which thematic context to attach.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Thematic paragraphs are keyed to universal historical facts (Council of Trent happened in 1545-1563, Bomberg printed the first Talmud in 1520-23). They describe the historical context, not the collection contents. The match_rules reference normalized field values that are stable. Annual review recommended but not urgent.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic paragraphs become stale if collection changes significantly

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: All thematic blocks are clearly labeled with '**Historical Context**:' header and separated by horizontal rule. Citations to reference works reinforce that this is scholarly commentary, not catalog metadata. The teaching note uses explicit 'Teaching note:' prefix.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Users confuse thematic context with catalog data

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Return only the highest-scoring theme (most matching dimensions). If tied, prefer the more specific theme (more match_rules). In the future, could return multiple blocks, but for E4 scope, single-theme is cleaner and avoids information overload.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Multiple themes match the same query (e.g., 'Talmud printed in Venice 16th century' matches both venetian_hebrew_printing and talmud_printing)

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Respect _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold. For sets >100, skip significance scoring entirely (return empty notable items list). The batched DB queries ensure at most 2 additional queries per response. Benchmarked: ~8ms for 100 records on SQLite.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Significance scoring is expensive for large result sets

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Theme matching is pure Python dict lookup against the filters -- no DB or API calls. ~0.1ms per match evaluation. Significance scoring involves 2 DB queries but only for result sets <=100 and only if a theme matched. Total additional latency: <15ms in worst case.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic context adds latency to every query response

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: This is by design. The 8 themes are chosen to address the specific 7 queries flagged in the historian evaluation. The system gracefully returns None when no theme matches (existing behavior preserved). New themes can be added incrementally as simple dict entries -- no code changes needed.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Only 8 themes authored initially -- many queries will not match

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Teaching notes only appear when the thematic block includes a teaching_note (not all themes need one). The note is factual and suggestive ('This set illustrates...'), not prescriptive. Future: could be gated by a user preference flag.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Pedagogical framing may feel inappropriate for non-academic users

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: significance_score() is designed as a standalone pure function with a clean interface (Candidate + db_path -> SignificanceResult). No internal state, no side effects. E5 can import and extend it. The SignificanceFactor model provides extensibility -- E5 can add new factor types without modifying E4 code.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: E5 (curation) depends on significance_score() from this module

fields.matched_by: List[str] -- which filter dimensions triggered the match (e.g., ['place:venice', 'year:1500-1600'])
fields.title: str -- display title (e.g., 'Venetian Hebrew Printing and Censorship')

fields.author: str -- e.g., 'Heller, Marvin J.'
fields.title: str -- e.g., 'The Sixteenth Century Hebrew Book'

fields.date_end: Optional[int] -- maximum year for date range overlap
fields.date_start: Optional[int] -- minimum year for date range overlap
fields.require_all: bool -- if True, all values must appear in filters (AND); if False, any value suffices (OR). Default: False

fields.block: ThematicBlock -- the content to return when matched
fields.match_rules: List[MatchRule] -- conditions that must be met to trigger this theme
fields.min_match_score: int -- minimum number of match_rules that must pass. Default: 1

schemaChanges.change: Add field: thematic_context: Optional[str] = None after line 201. Non-breaking -- optional field with None default.
